question: What is the structure of a `charmcraft.yaml` file for a Kubernetes charm? Show a complete example with all required fields.

claude_output.result:
# `charmcraft.yaml` for a Kubernetes Charm

Here's a complete example with all required fields and common optional sections:

```yaml
# charmcraft.yaml

# Required: The name of the charm (must match the registered name on Charmhub)
name: my-app

# Required: Type of charm
type: charm

# Required: Human-readable title
title: My Application

# Required: Short description
summary: A charm that deploys My Application on Kubernetes.

# Required: Longer description
description: |
  This charm deploys and manages My Application on Kubernetes.
  It supports scaling, configuration, and integration with
  databases and ingress controllers.

# Required: Bases/platforms the charm can run on
# For charmcraft >= 3.x, use `base` and `platforms`:
base: ubuntu@24.04
platforms:
  amd64:

# Required: At least one container for Kubernetes charms
containers:
  my-app:
    resource: oci-image

# Required: OCI image resource declaration (pairs with containers)
resources:
  oci-image:
    type: oci-image
    description: OCI image for the application
    upstream-source: docker.io/myorg/my-app:latest

# Optional but very common: relations/integrations
requires:
  database:
    interface: postgresql_client
  ingress:
    interface: ingress
    limit: 1

provides:
  metrics-endpoint:
    interface: prometheus_scrape
  grafana-dashboard:
    interface: grafana_dashboard

peers:
  replicas:
    interface: my_app_replica

# Optional: Configuration options
config:
  options:
    log-level:
      type: string
      default: info
      description: "Log level (debug, info, warning, error)"
    port:
      type: int
      default: 8080
      description: "Port the application listens on"

# Optional: Storage
storage:
  data:
    type: filesystem
    minimum-size: 1G
    location: /data

# Optional: Actions
actions:
  backup:
    description: "Create a backup of the application data"
    params:
      filename:
        type: string
        description: "Name of the backup file"

# Optional: Extra bindings
extra-bindings:
  public:

# Optional: Assumes (features the model must support)
assumes:
  - k8s-api
  - juju >= 3.1

# Optional: Charm libraries used
parts:
  charm:
    build-packages: []
    charm-python-packages: []
```

## Key Fields Explained

| Field | Required? | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `name` | ✅ | Unique charm name |
| `type` | ✅ | Must be `charm` |
| `summary` | ✅ | One-line description |
| `description` | ✅ | Full description |
| `base` + `platforms` | ✅ | Target OS and architectures |
| `containers` | ✅ (K8s) | Workload containers (what makes it a **K8s charm**) |
| `resources` | ✅ (K8s) | OCI image references for each container |
| `requires/provides/peers` | Optional | Relation endpoints |
| `config` | Optional | User-configurable options |
| `storage` | Optional | Persistent storage declarations |
| `actions` | Optional | Operator-triggered actions |
| `assumes` | Optional | Declare required Juju features |

## What makes it a Kubernetes charm?

The presence of the **`containers`** key is what distinguishes a Kubernetes charm from a machine charm. Each container entry maps to a Pebble-managed OCI container in the workload pod. The `resource` field links it to an entry under `resources` of `type: oci-image`.

> **Note:** If you're using **charmcraft 2.x** (older), replace `base`/`platforms` with:
> ```yaml
> bases:
>   - build-on:
>       - name: ubuntu
>         channel: "22.04"
>     run-on:
>       - name: ubuntu
>         channel: "22.04"
> ```
